model_narrative: We design a set of dice whose faces carry numeric values, arranged in a fixed cycle so that each die is paired against the next die in the cycle. For each die we choose the value on every face, and we decide, for each pairing of one of its faces against a face of the next die, whether that face is counted as a win. There is also a single common win count shared across the cycle. The objective is to maximize that common number of wins each die achieves against the next die.

sets.name: f
sets.doc: the faces on a single die, listed in increasing order; every die has this same set of face positions

sets.name: dice
sets.doc: the dice being designed, listed in a fixed cyclic order; the die after the last one wraps back to the first, so each die has a well-defined next die in the cycle

params.name: flo
params.doc: the lowest allowed value that may be assigned to any face of any die; a scalar equal to 1

params.name: fup
params.doc: the highest allowed value that may be assigned to any face of any die; a scalar equal to 18. The span of allowed values from lowest to highest, widened by one, serves as the large constant that switches the winning-relation inequalities on or off

vars.name: wnx
vars.doc: the common number of face-versus-face wins that every die scores against the next die in the cycle

vars.name: fval
vars.doc: the value printed on each face of each die; bounded between the lowest and highest allowed face values. The first face of the first die is fixed to the lowest allowed value to break symmetry

vars.name: comp
vars.doc: a win indicator over a die, one of its faces, and a face of the next die in the cycle; equals 1 when that face of the die is counted as beating that face of the next die, and 0 otherwise

constraints.name: comp[d,
constraints.intent: For each die, the total number of winning face matchups it scores against the next die in the cycle must equal the single common win count shared by all the dice. Add up the win indicators over all pairings of that die's faces against the next die's faces and set that total equal to the common number of wins.

constraints.name: dice)
constraints.intent: Whenever one of a die's faces is counted as beating a face of the next die in the cycle, that die's face value must be strictly greater than the other face's value, meaning at least one larger. This must hold for every combination of a die, one of its faces, and a face of the next die. When a matchup is not counted as a win the requirement must be allowed to fall away, so relax it using the available span of face values widened by one whenever the win indicator for that matchup is off.

constraints.name: f)
constraints.intent: On any single die the face values must be strictly increasing from one face to the next in order, with each face at least one greater than the face before it. For every die and every face other than its first, the value on that face must be at least one more than the value on the immediately preceding face of the same die.

query: The molds for the first die can only hold so much engraving, so there's a proposal on the table to keep the combined pip count across all six of die1's faces modest — no more than the square of the face count minus the face count itself, which works out to thirty here. With die1's total engraving budget held at that ceiling, what win count does the optimizer come back with?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_d1_total = Constraint(expr=sum(model.fval['dice1', f] for f in model.f) <= len(model.f)**2 - len(model.f))

query: There's an idea floating around to make the first die look less dominant in the head-to-head tables — the thought is to let it win no more than fifteen of its thirty-six face-versus-face matchups against the next die in the cycle. If we hold the count of die1's winning face comparisons at fifteen, what win count does the design settle on?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_d1_wins = Constraint(expr=sum(model.comp['dice1', f, fp] for f in model.f for fp in model.f) <= 15)

query: We're looking at a readability standard that would apply to every die in the set the same way: on any one die, the gap between its highest face and its lowest face shouldn't exceed ten pips, so the numbers stay in a tight, easy-to-scan band. With that span limit imposed uniformly on all three dice, what does the optimizer return for the per-die win count?

query: The board is pitching a 'lead die' concept where the first die clearly carries the most weight on the table. The framing is that die1's faces, added all together, should out-total die2's faces by a comfortable margin — at least ten pips clear once you sum up each die's six faces. Plug that lead-margin in and walk me through what win count the optimizer lands on.
expected_pyomo: model.d1_total_lead = Constraint(expr=sum(model.fval['dice1', f] for f in model.f) >= sum(model.fval['dice2', f] for f in model.f) + 10)

query: Both die1 and die3 sprawl across a huge range — die1 runs from 1 all the way to 16, die3 from 3 up to 18, and that wide spread makes the set look inconsistent. Couldn't we just tighten both of those dice so each one's top face sits within ten pips of its own bottom face? Apply that ten-pip span limit to die1 and die3 together and show me what the model's giving up by letting them stretch out — what's the tradeoff I'm missing?
expected_pyomo:
model.compress_d1 = Constraint(expr=model.fval['dice1', 'face6'] - model.fval['dice1', 'face1'] <= 10)
model.compress_d3 = Constraint(expr=model.fval['dice3', 'face6'] - model.fval['dice3', 'face1'] <= 10)

query: Die1's three highest faces are doing all the heavy lifting — they pile up near the top while its low faces barely matter. Wouldn't it be obvious to stop that top-heavy clustering by keeping die1's three largest faces, added together, under four times the face count — twenty-four pips across those top three? Cap that high-face cluster and tell me what's keeping the model from spreading die1 out that way.

query: On die3 the numbers come in uneven steps — some neighbouring faces sit right next to each other while others jump way apart, and it reads as sloppy. Shouldn't each face on die3 be at least two pips above the face just below it, so the values climb in clean, even steps? Impose that minimum two-pip step between consecutive faces on die3 and walk me through what's pulling the model away from laying it out that cleanly.
expected_pyomo:
def _gap_rule(model, f):
    fl = list(model.f)
    i = fl.index(f)
    return model.fval['dice3', f] >= model.fval['dice3', fl[i - 1]] + 2 if i > 0 else Constraint.Skip
model.spread_d3 = Constraint(model.f, rule=_gap_rule)
